title: Birakati Mohan Rao vs Kandregula Hima Prasad on 29 September, 2022
body: Birakati Mohan Rao vs Kandregula Hima Prasad on 29 September, 2022 THE HON'BLE Ms. JUSTICE B.S.BHANUMATHI Appeal Suit No.357 of 2017 JUDGMENT: This appeal, under Section 96 CPC, is preferred by the unsuccessful plaintiff aggrieved by the judgment and decree, dated 15.06.2016, passed in O.S.No.359 of 2013 on the file of the Court of XIII Additional District Judge, Visakhapatnam. 2. Heard Sri K. Srinivas, learned counsel representing Sri K. Gani Reddy, learned counsel for the appellant/plaintiff and Sri V. Surendra Reddy, learned counsel for the respondent/ defendant. 3. The case of the plaintiff, in brief, is that the defendant borrowed a sum of Rs.20,00,000/- from the plaintiff on 02.10.2012 for the purpose of discharging his sundry debts and on the even date, the defendant executed a demand promissory note in favour of the plaintiff agreeing to repay the same together with interest @ 24% per annum either to the plaintiff or his order on demand. In spite of repeated demands made by the plaintiff, the defendant has not paid either the principal or the interest accrued thereon and has been postponing the repayment on one pretext or the other. The defendant is not an agriculturist and hence, he is not entitled for the 2 BSB, J A.S.No.357 of 2017 benefit under Act IV of 1938, Act VII of 1977. Hence, the plaintiff is constrained to file the suit. (b) The defendant filed written statement denying the plaint averments and resisting the suit. The plaintiff is a low rank employee in Steel Plant and has no financial source to lend a huge amount of Rs.20,00,000/-. About two years back, the defendant borrowed only an amount of Rs.50,000/-. As usual, the plaintiff obtained signature of this defendant on two or three blank promissory notes apart from obtaining two blank cheques. The defendant paid interest for a long time and also paid some portion of the principal amount borrowed. For the last one year, the defendant got stuck in financial crisis and could not discharge his obligations towards his creditors including the plaintiff. The defendant also sought concession from the plaintiff seeking waiver of interest on the principal borrowed. The plaintiff did not agree and on the other hand, warned this defendant of filing suits for higher amounts making use of blank promissory notes. The plaintiff filed the present suit fabricating one of the blank promissory notes given by the defendant to the plaintiff. There is no cause of action to file the suit and the cause of action mentioned in the plaint is invented. The suit is liable to be dismissed. 3 BSB, J A.S.No.357 of 2017 4. On the basis of the above pleadings, the trial Court framed the following issues for trial: (i) Whether the plaintiff is entitled for recovery of suit amount of Rs.23,60,000/- from the defendant with subsequent interest @ 24% per annum from the date of filing of the suit till the date of realization as prayed for? (ii) Whether the suit promissory note is a fabricated one as pleaded by the defendant ? (iii) To what relief? 5. On behalf of the plaintiff, PWs 1 to 3 were examined and exhibit A1 is marked. On behalf of the defendant, the defendant himself was examined as DW 1 and got examined one more witness, who is an employee in the Steel Plant, as DW2. Exhibits X1 and X2 were marked on behalf of the defendant. 6. After hearing the submissions of both sides, the trial Court dismissed the suit by the impugned judgment. 7. The aggrieved plaintiff filed this appeal. The appellant/plaintiff mainly contended in the grounds of appeal that the evidence of PWs 1 to 3 would clearly establish the fact that the respondent/ defendant borrowed an amount of Rs.20,00,000/- and executed exhibit A1, promissory note, in favour of the appellant/plaintiff and as the respondent/defendant himself admitted his signature on exhibit A1, presumption under Section 118 of the Negotiable instruments Act applies to the case of the appellant. The 4 BSB, J A.S.No.357 of 2017 respondent/defendant could not prove the contention that the promissory note is a fabricated document. The reasoning given by the trial Court and the findings arrived thereon are unsustainable. The judgment of the trial Court is based on assumptions and surmises. If the judgment and decree of the trial Court are allowed to stand, it would occasion in failure of justice. The trial Court erred in dismissing the suit. 8. Learned counsel for the appellant/plaintiff contended that the plaintiff has discharged his initial burden by examining himself as a witness and also one of the attestors and the scribe of the promissory note, but the defendant failed to discharge his onus in order to show that the promissory note is not supported by consideration, yet, the trial Court erred in dismissing the suit. He placed reliance on the decision of the Supreme Court in Rao Saheb Dattatraya v. Ranganath Gopalrao Kawathekar 1 , wherein it was held at paragaraph No.5 as follows: "........Mr. Sanghi, the learned Counsel for the Appellant contended that when the execution of a document is denied, the party seeking to prove that document must not only prove that the alleged executants has signed that deed, but he must also prove that the executants had signed the same with the knowledge of its contents, What facts and circumstances have to be established to prove 1 1971 Law Suit (SC) 93 5 BSB, J A.S.No.357 of 2017 the execution of a document depend on the pleas put forward. If the only plea taken is that the executant has not signed the document and that the document is a forgery, party seeking to prove the execution of a document need not adduce evidence to show that the party who signed the document knew the contents of the document. Ordinarily, no one is expected to sign a document without knowing its contents, but, if it is pleaded that the party who signed the document did not know the contents of the document, then, it may, in certain circumstances, be necessary for the party seeking to prove the document to place material before the Court to satisfy it that the party who signed the document had the knowledge of its contents." (b) He further placed reliance on the decision of the Supreme Court in Bharat Barrel & Drum Manufacturing Company v. Amin Chand Payrelal2 at paragraph No.12, it was held as follows: "........Once execution of the promissory note is admitted, the presumption under Section 118(a) would arise that it is supported by consideration. Such a presumption is rebuttable. The defendant can prove the non- existence of consideration by raising a probable defence. If the defendant is proved to have discharged the initial onus of proof showing that the existence of consideration was improbable or doubtful or the same was illegal, the onus would shift to the plaintiff who will be obliged to prove it as a matter of fact and upon its failure to prove would 2 (1999) 3 Supreme Court Cases 35 6 BSB, J A.S.No.357 of 2017 disentitle him to the grant of relief on the basis of the negotiable instrument. The burden upon the defendant of proving the non-existence of the consideration can be either direct or by bringing on record the preponderance of probabilities by reference to the circumstances upon which he relies. In such an event the plaintiff is entitled under law to rely upon all the evidence led in the case including that of the plaintiff as well. In case, where the defendant fails to discharge the initial onus of proof by showing the non-existence of the consideration, the plaintiff would invariably be held entitled to the benefit of presumption arising under Section 118(a) in his favour. The court may not insist upon the defendant to disprove the existence of consideration by leading direct evidence as existence of negative evidence is neither possible nor contemplated and even if led is to be seen with a doubt. The bare denial of the passing of the consideration apparently does not appear to be any defence. Something which is probable has to be brought on record for getting the benefit of shifting the onus of proving to the plaintiff. To disprove the presumption the defendant has to bring on record such facts and circumstances, upon consideration of which the court may either believe that the consideration did not exist or its non-existence was so probable that a prudent man would, under the circumstances of the case, shall act upon the plea that it did not exist.." It was further held in the cited decision as under: "It would thus be clear that when the suit is based on pronote, and promissory note is proved to have been 7 BSB, J A.S.No.357 of 2017 executed, Section 118(a) raises the presumption, until the contrary is proved, that the promissory note was made for consideration. That initial presumption raised under Section 118(a) becomes unavailable when the plaintiff himself pleads in the plaint different considerations. If he pleads that the promissory note is supported by a consideration as recited in the negotiable instrument and the evidence adduced in support thereof, the burden is on the defendant to disprove that the promissory note is not supported by consideration or different consideration other than one recited in the promissory note did pass, if that consideration is not valid in law nor enforceable in law, the court would consider whether the suit pronote is supported by valid consideration or legally enforceable consideration. Take for instance, a pronote executed for a time barred debt. It is still a valid consideration. The falsity of the plea of the plaintiff also would be a factor to be considered by the Court. The burden of proof is of academic interest when the evidence was adduced by the parties. The court is required to examine the evidence and consider whether the suit as pleaded in the plaint has been established and the suit requires to be decreed or dismissed." (c) Learned counsel further placed reliance on the decision in Ramachandra Rambux v. Champabai and others 3, wherein at paragraph No.8, it was held as follows: "........In order to judge the credibility of the witnesses, the Court is not confined only to the way in which the 3 AIR 1965 SUPREME COURT 354 8 BSB, J A.S.No.357 of 2017 witnesses have deposed or to the demeanor of witnesses, but it is open to it to look into the surrounding circumstances as well as the probabilities, so that it may be able to form a correct idea of the trustworthiness of the witnesses. This issue cannot be determined by considering the evidence adduced in the Court separately from the surrounding circumstances which have also been brought out in the evidence, or which appear from the nature and contents of the document itself. We do not understand the observations of Lord Watson to mean that the testimony as to the execution of the document has to be considered independently of the attendant circumstances. All that he says is that where there is a large and consistent body of testimony tending to show the execution of a will by the testator, that evidence should not be lightly set aside on the theory of improbability." 9. On the other hand, learned counsel for the respondent/ defendant submitted that the defendant could discharge his onus by showing that the plaintiff has no capacity to lend such huge amount and thereby, it can be established that the suit promissory note was fabricated by using the promissory note signed by the defendant when he borrowed Rs.50,000/- in the past. 10. As can be seen from the pleadings and the evidence, it is an admitted fact that both the plaintiff and the defendant are known to each other. According to the defendant, he had a previous financial transaction with the plaintiff. The plaintiff is silent about the same. 9 BSB, J A.S.No.357 of 2017 The defendant could not place evidence showing the past transactions. Be that as it may, now the suit transaction is to be examined in the light of the pleadings and the evidence. 11. No doubt, it is true that the initial burden lies on the plaintiff when the defendant denies the suit transaction and the onus shifts to the defendant to prove the defence on discharge of the initial burden by the plaintiff. It is also settled law that when both parties lead evidence, the element of burden of proof loses its significance and all the evidence lead by both parties should be read together. In the decision relied on by the appellant's counsel, it is also observed that the credibility of the witnesses can be judged by not confining only to the way in which the witnesses have deposed, but it is open to the Court to look into the surrounding circumstances as well as the probabilities to take a correct view. 12. The defendant pleaded that he had handed over the plaintiff signed blank pronote and further that the suit pronote was fabricated. He did not plead it was forged. He did not deny his signature on exhibit A1 in his evidence. Thus, there is no dispute about the signature of the defendant on the suit promissory note. As per Section 20 of the Negotiable Instruments Act, 1881, where one person signs and delivers to another a paper stamped in accordance with the law relating to negotiable instruments, and 10 BSB, J A.S.No.357 of 2017 either wholly blank or having written thereon an incomplete negotiable instrument, he thereby gives prima facie authority to the holder thereof to make or complete, as the case may be, upon it a negotiable instrument, for any amount specified therein and not exceeding the amount covered by the stamp, the person so signing shall be liable upon such instrument, in the capacity in which he signed the same, to any holder in due course for such amount. In view thereof, the holder is authorized to fill the details. As per the decision in Rao Saheb Dattatraya (1 supra) relied on by the learned counsel for the appellant, though the signature is proved or admitted, when knowledge of the contents are denied by the defendant, in certain cases, the plaintiff may have to establish that the defendant has knowledge of the contents. This decision was rendered in relation to a document admitting share in property. So it is not in relation to a negotiable instrument. 13. The plaintiff relied on the oral evidence of PWs 1 to 3. Both PWs 2 & 3 spoke in favour of the plaintiff. Just because a witness speaks on oath, the same cannot be considered as gospel truth. The veracity of such evidence has to be examined in the light of other evidence on record. It is the contention of the defendant that PWs 2 & 3 supported the case of the plaintiff, though in fact, there is no such transaction took place. In this regard, it is his main contention that the plaintiff has no capacity to lend such huge 11 BSB, J A.S.No.357 of 2017 amount, and therefore, it is improbable that he borrowed such huge amount from the plaintiff as stated by PWs 1 to 3. Thus, the evidence of PWs 1 to 3 regarding execution of the suit promissory note as well as passing of consideration therein has to be evaluated in the light of the evidence lead by the defendant on the aspect of capacity of the plaintiff to lend the amount. 14. PW1 admitted that he was a Kalasi when he joined service and he used to draw salary of Rs.1100/- per month. He was a low salaried (standard sic) employee and further that he retired from service in November, 2014 and by then, his gross salary was Rs.15,000/- per month. He admitted to have only single source of income, that is, his salary and also specifically that he had no other monthly source of income. It is also admitted by him that he has no property in his name or in the name of his wife and that he has only one bank account in which his salary was deposited. Admittedly, he had to look after his two children for their education and living and all his family needs are met by him from his salary alone. He further stated that there was no balance amount of Rs.20 lakhs at a time at any point of time in his account. However, he stated that some times, an amount of Rs.15 lakhs, sometimes, an amount of Rs.10 lakhs and at times, a sum of Rs.5 lakhks were available in his account. Except his oral statement, no such proof is filed in spite of denial of his statement. Thus, it is clear that, being 12 BSB, J A.S.No.357 of 2017 a very low salaried employee, meeting all his family expenses from such salary alone, in the absence of any proof of any other source of having such huge amount, it is improbable that he had such huge amount of Rs.20 lakhs to lend. Furthermore, he admitted that he performed the marriage of his daughter on 01.11.2012 and that he received amount from his parents-in-law for the purpose of marriage of his daughter. Yet, he stated to have lent huge amount of Rs.20 lakhs to the defendant on 2nd October, 2012. The time gap between the suit document and the marriage is exactly a month. Though he stated that he stated to have taken a loan of Rs.5 lakhs from his P.F account, he did not produce any such evidence. Similarly, though he stated to have secured amount out of private chits to the extent of Rs.5 lakhs, no such evidence is placed. All this evidence put together shows that it is highly improbable that the plaintiff raised such huge amount to lend to the defendant on 02.10.2012. The trial Court also has rightly appreciated the evidence and came to a just conclusion that the plaintiff had no such capacity to pay consideration under exhibit A1. In the light of such admitted evidence of PW1 himself, the oral evidence of PWs 1 to 3 supporting the suit claim can be said as unworthy of any credence. Thus, the plaintiff could not prove the suit claim in view of the efforts made by the defendant in culling out evidence from 13 BSB, J A.S.No.357 of 2017 PW1 to disprove the case of this plaintiff. As such, this Court finds that there is no merit in the appeal. 15. In the result, the appeal is dismissed. There shall be no order as to costs. Miscellaneous petitions, if any, pending in this appeal shall stand closed. _________________ B. S. BHANUMATHI, J 29th September, 2022 RAR
